text: Laparoscopic Subtotal Cholecystectomy Operative Technique by Dr. T. Varun Raju A sixty-two-year-old female presented with long-standing pain at right upper quadrant of the abdomen with recurrent bouts of fever with a palpable lump at right hypochondrium. Primary consultant has done the complete biochemical radiological investigations with the conclusion of cholelithiasis with thickened gallbladder wall. Operative technique shown here, postoperative recovery was uneventful with no bile leak. Histopathology confirmed cholecystitis with no atypia.

The gallbladder is observed with mass formation involving the omentum and bowel. Dense adhesions are separated using a laparoscopic cannula, gently pushing the omentum downward. A spinal needle is inserted through the abdominal wall into the gallbladder to aspirate its contents, allowing the gallbladder wall to be grasped for cranial retraction. The laparoscopic cannula aids in separating the omentum from the gallbladder wall and aspirating any oozing during blunt dissection. A harmonic scalpel is used to dissect the remaining adhesions.

The surgeon observes the Calot's triangle tightly adherent to the stomach wall and notes the friability of the gallbladder wall at the infundibulum, leading to the decision to perform a subtotal cholecystectomy.

The surgeon begins dissection with a harmonic scalpel from the anterior surface of the infundibulum, continuing to the posterior side. A vertical slit is made in the gallbladder wall to retrieve an impacted large gallstone, which is carefully picked up and placed in a glove bag.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform posterior dissection, completely disconnecting the infundibulum from the body of the gallbladder. The infundibulum is visibly free from the gallbladder. A 1-0 V-lock suture is applied to close the stump of the infundibulum with continuous intracorporeal suturing, ensuring tight closure at the edges.

The surgeon uses laparoscopic scissors to cut the suture and remove the needle under direct vision. A laparoscopic L-hook instrument with monopolar diathermy is employed to carefully dissect the gallbladder from its liver bed, ensuring good hemostasis. The gallbladder specimen is then removed through the epigastric site. A drain is inserted through the right mid-axillary port and positioned at the gallbladder bed. A thorough saline wash is performed, and pneumoperitoneum is released after removing secondary ports under vision. The specimen containing the stone is sent for histopathology.
